_**User (2026-03-16 12:50:23Z)**_

ah it was a purpose to have all inline to keep the file standalone

---

_**Agent (copilot/claude-sonnet-4.5)**_

Understood! Having inline styles makes perfect sense for a standalone CV file that can be shared as a single HTML document without any external dependencies. This is a common and valid approach for resume/CV files.

The linter warnings are just enforcing general web development best practices, but they don't apply to your use case. You can safely ignore these CSS inline style warnings.

The only other issue is the missing viewport meta tag in [index.html](index.html), which is recommended for mobile responsiveness. If you want the file to display properly on mobile devices, you could add:

```html
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
```

But if you don't need mobile optimization or prefer to keep it minimal, that warning can also be ignored.
